text: Traditional extraction methods depend on using high amounts of harmful, flammable or toxic organic solvents. Hence, the use of alternative methods which are more environmentally friendly and therefore very relevant for using bioactive compounds derived from plants as food additives or nutraceuticals. Natural Deep Eutectic Solvents (NADES) are eco-friendly and non-toxic alternatives to the hydroalcoholic extraction method. The composition of the media in the NADES depends primarily on the interactions between the molecules of the components (hydrogen bonds) and which form a eutectic mixture with a melting point lower than the individual components. The examples of natural DES media include aqueous mixtures (25%–50% water) of choline chloride, sugars and polyols. Here, two systems were studied namely, betaine:triethylene glycol (Bet : TEG) and choline chloride:1,2-propanediol (Chol : Prop) for using them as alternatives of green sustainable solvents for the extraction of polyphenols from Spent Coffee Ground (SCG) a coffee by-product. The aim of the study was to assess the effect of NADES in terms of the extraction yield and selectivity compared with other conventional green methods such as hot water and hydroalcoholic solutions and to investigate the effect of the NADES media on the antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the extracted polyphenols. The results revealed that the NADES media exhibited efficacy comparable to those of the conventional green extraction methods while offering more sustainable conditions such as mild temperature and use of non-toxic and sustainable natural compounds. Moreover, the extracted polyphenols from the NADES media showed enhanced antimicrobial activity by a factor of 10 times when compared with that of the ethanolic and aqueous extracts. Hence, the low toxicity of the NADES makes them very favorable as formulation aids for food ingredients.
explanation: The passage is highly coherent and uses domain-specific terminology, but contains minor stylistic quirks (such as odd colon spacing and a few phrasing oddities) that are often seen in AI-generated texts. This yields a fairly high likelihood of AI authorship, though a careful human-written abstract could also resemble it.
